narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 28.50, your profile also includes being in the 2nd decile for Year 3 school grades, the 4th decile for Year 1 grades, and coming from an upper-middle income family while planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated your probability of failing at 49.9%—a risk high enough to lead to the rejection of your law school application. This threshold reflects the school's aim to prioritize applicants most likely to succeed on the bar, ensuring strong outcomes for their graduates. The key factor driving this prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile, which ranked quite low at 2 compared to the typical student's 5.53 average; this stood out as the strongest signal pushing the model toward forecasting bar failure, as weaker performance in later years often correlates with exam struggles in similar students. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, while slightly below the 3.21 average, worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward success, showing the model viewed it as a relative strength. Similarly, even though your LSAT score of 28.50 is well below the 36.70 average, it still contributed positively by slightly countering the failure risk, unlike cases where extremely low scores dominate. Compared to typical admitted students, who often have higher deciles across the board and stronger test scores, your Year 3 performance created the biggest gap, outweighing your other solid aspects like family background and full-time commitment. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from an upper-middle income family—many in similar demographics succeed with stronger late-stage academics—but the model weighs these patterns heavily to minimize bar failure rates. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average, strengthening your Year 3 record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and reapplying to schools with more flexible thresholds. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early, as data shows they help students with profiles like yours overcome predicted risks. With focused improvements, your baseline strengths could tip the scales next time. Keep pushing; many applicants turn rejections into success stories by addressing these exact levers.
